author: polariscampus
text: We’ve launched the Polaris All India Scholarship. Here’s the complete breakdown.

author: polariscampus
text:
Wherever you’re starting your B.Tech in 2026, we will BACK YOU! 🌠

Introducing the All India Polaris Scholarship.

Ten students, selected from across the country, backed at the very beginning of their BTech journey with something real.

Rank 1 to 3 win ₹2,00,000 each - cash they can use however their journey demands. Fees, tools, courses, breathing room. You name it.

Rank 4 to 10 win a MacBook each - the most powerful laptop in the world, theirs from day one of engineering college.

Here’s how it works:
 → Register for the Polaris Aptitude Test before 15th July
 → Top 500 write a scholarship essay
 → Top 50 move to a video interview
 → 10 winners. 3 get ₹2 lakh. 7 get a MacBook.

If you’re starting BTech in 2026 and are serious about building a future for yourself, registration link for the Polaris Aptitude Test (PAT) is in the comments and in our bio. Apply now!

🧷Link in BIO & COMMENTS! 

#AllIndiaPolaris Scholarship #Polaris #EngineeringIndia #BTech2026 #Scholarship

author: polariscampus
text:
Another world-class partner joins The Polaris Fellowship.

Welcome, Wispr Flow.

Together, we’re giving the next generation of builders access to the tools they need to do their best work.

Link in Bio.

author: himeeshmadaan
text:
In today’s AI era, the biggest fear is not just losing jobs… it’s losing direction.

Mukul Rustagi opens up about a world where degrees are no longer enough, tech jobs are changing fast, and an entire generation is confused about what to learn, which career to choose, and where money will be made in the next 5–10 years.

In this powerful podcast, he shares:

How AI is changing the future of tech jobs and why being “cheap talent” is no longer enough
Why YouTube and ChatGPT can become the two biggest tools for young people to build their future
How students can use skills, proof of work, and global opportunities to reach their first ₹1 crore
Why the next wave of wealth may come from solopreneurs, AI ventures, and product builders
The mistakes young entrepreneurs make while building ideas, companies, and wealth

This isn’t just a conversation about AI or technology.

It’s a roadmap for every young person who feels confused about career, money, skills, and the future.

Watch the full conve

author: polariscampus
text:
Kids These Days- Episode 9

Every home has asked the same question:

“Aaj kya banayein?”

So Ishaan and Manas built an AI that looks inside your fridge and tells you exactly what to cook.

author: polariscampus
text:
Kids These Days- Episode 8

Most people ignore scam texts.

Soham built an AI that reads them for you, scores the risk, and explains exactly why they’re dangerous.

author: polariscampus
text:
What impressed Aayush Gupta, a founding team member at FAM, wasn’t just the ideas it was the questions.

From startup validation to RBI compliance, Polaris students were thinking beyond products and exploring how businesses are actually built.

His biggest takeaway? Curiosity is one of the most valuable skills a builder can develop.

Because in a world where AI can execute, asking better questions becomes the real advantage.

author: polariscampus
text:
Most legal AI tools help lawyers research.

This one goes a step further.

Built by a Polaris student, LexForge helps lawyers build arguments, challenge them through a devil’s advocate mode, and even simulate courtroom debates.

The goal isn’t to replace lawyers, it’s to help them prepare better before stepping into court.

A fascinating example of AI solving real-world problems with privacy and trust at its core.

author: polariscampus
text:
Kids These Days — Episode 7

Most students use AI to answer questions.

Rehan built an AI that studies with you.

Upload your notes and it turns them into summaries, quizzes, flashcards, and even podcasts.

author: polariscampus
text:
The best way to evaluate a college isn’t through brochures or rankings.

It’s by visiting the campus, talking to the students, meeting the mentors, and seeing how learning actually happens.

Sometimes, that’s all the clarity you need.

author: polariscampus
text:
Kids These Days- Episode 6

Most travel apps tell you where to go.

Sakshi and Manav built one that tells you the story behind where you already are.

Meet Loci, an AI travel companion that turns every city into a guided tour.

author: polariscampus
text:
Comment FELLOWSHIP and we’ll send you the details in DM. 

Work with founders. Meet operators. Build real things. Bain & Company, Blume, Emergent, and more.

This is Polaris Fellowship.

author: polariscampus
text: He met founders of billion dollar companies because he was creating content. Not because he had connections. It took 40 podcasts before one even got 10,000 views but the doors it opened were worth every single one. A job gives you a linear path. Content gives you asymmetric returns.

author: polariscampus
text:
Most students wait until their final year to start building a career. Polaris students are doing it in Year 1.

₹1 Cr+ in stipends, 109 opportunities, 35 global open-source selections, and internships across 11 countries.

This isn’t a placement report. It’s what happens when you focus on proof of work from Day 1.

author: polariscampus
text:
Comment Link to get the project link in your DMs.

He spent months in 12th grade just trying to figure out which college to pick. Fees on one site, placements on another, rankings buried in some PDF. 

So he built an AI that does all of it in seconds. Ask it anything about any college, compare two colleges side by side, or upload a brochure and it reads the whole thing for you. 

If the data is not verified, it refuses to answer. Kids These Days, Episode 5.

author: polariscampus
text:
Sehajdeep Kaur, a first year student@Polaris has been selected as a delegate for the HPAIR (Harvard Project for Asian and International Relations) Asia Conference 2026 - a prestigious global conference founded by Harvard students that brings together future leaders from across the world.

This year’s conference will be held in Hanoi, Vietnam, where some of the brightest, and most exceptional students will participate in keynote sessions, executive seminars, policy debates, workshops, networking events, and the renowned Impact Challenge alongside peers from dozens of countries.

Since its founding in 1991, HPAIR has connected more than 40,000 delegates across conferences focused on leadership, global affairs, technology, business, policy, and international collaboration.

For us, it’s a true testament to our belief that when you place ambitious students in the right rooms, surround them with the right opportunities, and trust them with responsibility early, extraordinary things tend to

author: polariscampus
text:
You can read about great companies or you can sit inside them. 

The Polaris Fellowship gives 10 student builders a chance to build across Bain, Blume, InMobi, Classplus, Emergent, and more for 6 months. 

Applications close June 28. Only 10 fellows will be selected nationwide. Comment FELLOWSHIP for the application link.

author: polariscampus
text: Two Essential Skills Every Student Needs in 2026.

author: polariscampus
text:
Polaris at World’s Largest Education Conference. 

NAFSA 2026 (the world’s largest international education conference) with over 10,000 educators from over 150 countries in attendance, just wrapped up in Orlando. 

Our Head of Academics, Avinash Ramakanth, was there, representing Polaris on one of the most prominent stages in global education. 
And this year’s theme couldn’t have felt more personal to us.

“Global by Design.”

Not global as a goal you work toward or as something you earn after four years and a visa application. Global as a decision - baked in from the very beginning.

Because for every student who walks into Polaris - You don’t chase global after you graduate. You don’t wait for a fellowship, a lucky break, or someone’s permission.

You get it on Day 1.

Real projects, global teams, production-grade work - all in your first year, not your final one.

And that’s not a promise. It’s already happening. Check out our website to know this year’s global internships and selec

author: polariscampus
text:
Day 2 at VibeCon surrounded by people building ambitious things.

The energy in the room was unreal.

Bangalore Zonals - HackArena 2.0 🔥

12th June'26 saw an immense wave of developers who brought onto the stage ideation and innovation ✨

@polariscampus @igniteroom.india

author: polariscampus
text:
He was too scared for a mock interview. So he built an AI that does the interviewing for you. 

Kids These Days, Episode 4. Upload your resume, pick the role, and it interviews you out loud like a real hiring manager. When you are done, it shows you exactly where you stumbled and how to fix it. 

Built in first year. From one nervous moment.

author: rustagi_mukul
text:
The Linux Foundation Mentorship. 

One of the most respected open source programs in the world.

13 students just got in. ₹5.7 lakh highest stipend. 12 of them haven’t even finished first year.

author: polariscampus
text:
You spend years preparing to get into college.

Almost nobody teaches you how to choose the right one.

This Saturday, Lucky Jethani (IIT Bombay PG, AIR 46 GATE) shares how he’d choose a college if he had to do it all over again.

Link in bio.

author: polariscampus
text:
Kids These Days is a series where we show you the crazy things students at Polaris are building.

Episode 3: Raj.

Growing up in a family of gynaecologists, Raj saw how confusing women’s healthcare can be. 

So he built MomPulse- an AI companion that supports women through every stage, from their first period to pregnancy and beyond.

A problem he grew up around. A product built to solve it.

author: polariscampus
text:
A day full of excitement, talent, and unforgettable memories! 

Freshers’ Day 2026 at Polaris School of Technology brought together new faces, fresh energy, and incredible performances. 

From ramp walks to electrifying stage acts, every moment was worth celebrating. 

Here’s to new friendships, new beginnings, and an amazing journey ahead!

author: polariscampus
text:
Kids These Days is a series where we show you the crazy things students at Polaris are building.

Episode 2: Deep.

He watched his friends spend 12 hours a day preparing for NEET PG and still struggle to retain what they learned. So he built AORTA — an AI study partner that reads the textbook with you, quizzes you, tracks your weak spots, and connects concepts across subjects.

He may never take the exam himself. But he just built the study companion every medical student wishes they had.

author: polariscampus
text:
India’s biggest AI student fellowship is here. Applications open now! 📣

When the most ambitious student builders in the country stop optimizing for internships and start optimizing for impact….

They don’t need just another credential, they need access.

Access to ambitious companies, founders, operators, access to problems worth solving.

That’s why we created the Polaris Fellowship: A six-month residential experience where 10 exceptional students will live, work, and build alongside some of India’s most ambitious companies. 

Backed by ₹1 Crore in Fellow Stipends. 

Link in bio.

author: polariscampus
text:
What is the strongest tangible proof of coding competence in real-world scenarios?

Open Source it is. Which is strongly engraved into the culture of Polaris.

This year, 18 Polaris students became part of that culture through Google Summer of Code 2026.

And they’re not just contributing locally. They’re now working across open-source ecosystems spanning 9 countries, contributing to real-world systems in:
- cybersecurity
- healthcare & genomics
- developer infrastructure
- ⁠geospatial systems
- ⁠programming languages

Working alongside experienced mentors, building systems used by developers and organizations across the world.

But behind every selection is a completely different story.
While some students started with utmost clarity, some rebuilt from scratch after setbacks. Some learned by breaking things, fixing them, and trying again.
What connects all of them is simple:
They showed up consistently.
 They kept building.

And they contributed before they felt “ready.” That’s what o
